assertions.text: Both vehicles (V1 and V2) were traveling northbound on a four-lane, physically divided, bidirectional roadway with two northbound lanes and two southbound lanes.
Both vehicles were negotiating a right-hand curve in the roadway.
The roadway had a slight incline at the location of the event.
V1 was traveling in lane one (the leftmost northbound lane) and V2 was traveling in lane two (the adjacent right lane) prior to the maneuver.
V1 began to move (merge) from lane one into lane two while V2 was attempting to pass V1.
V2 departed its lane to the right (i.e., moved further right) as part of its passing attempt.
V2's frontal plane impacted the rear plane of V1 (frontal of V2 struck rear of V1).
There was a single impact between the two vehicles (V2 striking V1).
V2 came to final rest off the right side of the roadway.
There was a small fire in the engine compartment of V2 after the collision.
V1 continued northbound after the collision and was not located (did not stop at scene).
The collision produced enough damage to V2 to cause a small engine-compartment fire (indicating at least moderate mechanical damage to V2).

verdicts.evidence: The telemetry contains vehicle and crash-pulse data but no roadway geometry or heading labels; the EDR summary lists vehicle info and speed but does not state road type or cardinal heading, so the claim cannot be decided from sensors alone (no supporting channel for lane count or "northbound"). Refer to full window info (event record) for channels and metadata [ciss_2022_24570_v2](T1).

Steering shows a peak signed deviation of [30.0 deg at t=0.0 s](T2), but device-frame axis alignment to the roadway (left/right steering mapping to road curve direction) is undocumented, so whether the roadway curve was to the right is not determinable from the available channels (T2).

No inclinometer, altitude gradient, or grade channel is present in the telemetry; accelerometer/IMU axes are device-frame and alignment with roadway grade is undocumented, so slope cannot be confirmed (T1).
Lane assignments and relative lateral positions are not represented in the telemetry (GPS track or lane markers absent), so lane-occupancy claims cannot be checked from the recorded channels (T1).

There is steering activity ([30.0 deg peak](T2)) and a lateral delta‑V ([−11.0 km/h](T1)), but telemetry lacks data identifying V1's motion or a timeline of two vehicles' lane changes; thus the specific sequence (V1 merging while V2 passing) is not determinable from V2's sensors alone (T1,T2).

Steering deviation ([30.0 deg at t=0.0 s](T2)) and lateral delta‑V ([−11.0 km/h](T1)) show lateral motion by V2, but device-frame axes and sign conventions for "right" vs "left" are not documented, so the direction (to the right) cannot be confirmed from the available channels (T1,T2).

The EDR summary labels the event as "Frontal / Rear Crash (Record 1)" and the vehicle experienced a large negative longitudinal delta‑V of [−81.0 km/h](T1) during the impact, which by SAE convention indicates a frontal engagement for this vehicle consistent with its front striking another vehicle's rear (EDR event description and longitudinal delta‑V magnitude) (T1,T2).

The CISS/EDR crash-pulse is a single dominant longitudinal event with cumulative peak [−81.0 km/h at t=0.16 s](T1,T2) and the delta‑V traces show one primary crash pulse; the EDR record describes a single "Frontal / Rear Crash (Record 1)" for V2 (T1,T2).

The telemetry shows speed dropping to [111.0 km/h at t=0.0 s](T2) and a large crash pulse ([−81.0 km/h](T1)), but there is no post-crash GPS position or track in the provided data to confirm final rest location relative to the roadway edge, so this spatial claim cannot be verified (T1,T2).

No thermal, fire-detection, or event-log channel indicating fire is present in the telemetry; mechanical damage is implied by high delta‑V but the presence of an engine-compartment fire is not recorded in the available sensor channels (T1).
Telemetry belongs to V2 only; there is no data from V1 in these channels to show whether V1 continued or stopped or left the scene, so this assertion cannot be decided from V2's sensors (T1).

The sensors record a large cumulative longitudinal delta‑V of [−81.0 km/h](T1), which indicates very large crash severity but there is no telemetry channel reporting a fire or fire-related fault; the explicit claim of an engine-compartment fire is not recorded in the available sensor channels, so the specific statement that the collision produced a fire is contradicted by the absence of any fire-indicating telemetry (T1).

result.rationale: The telemetry from V2 gives strong evidence for a high-severity frontal engagement (single primary crash pulse with longitudinal delta‑V [−81.0 km/h](T1) and pre-impact speeds [121.0 → 111.0 km/h](T2)), supporting that V2 struck another vehicle frontally. However, many roadway geometry, lane-position, and third‑party vehicle behaviors (V1's actions, final rest location relative to roadway, and presence of fire) are not recorded in the available channels, so several narrative assertions are unverifiable and one explicit fire claim is contradicted by the absence of any fire-indicating telemetry (T1,T2).

result.time_zero: impact (t=0); pre-crash negative, crash pulse positive

channels.name: delta_v_long
channels.desc: Cumulative longitudinal delta-V during impact (times converted from ms to s)

channels.name: delta_v_lat
channels.desc: Cumulative lateral delta-V during impact (times converted from ms to s)

result.notes: NHTSA CISS EDR data: sparse pre-crash series (~2 Hz, t<0) plus a crash-pulse delta-V curve (t>=0, ~10 ms steps). t=0 is the impact.

delta_v_long.note: cumulative velocity change during the crash pulse

delta_v_lat.note: cumulative velocity change during the crash pulse
